summary: The paper presents a cross-architecture benchmark (CAAB) for feature absorption in SAEs, with honest reporting of mostly null results. While the manuscript demonstrates exemplary transparency about limitations, several critical methodological and interpretive issues remain: (1) the core architecture comparison confounds architecture with training data/width/dictionary size, making the 4x collision rate difference uninterpretable; (2) all three primary hypotheses (H2-H4) are falsified, yet the paper still frames CAAB as a meaningful contribution rather than a failed experiment; (3) collision rate is a weak proxy for true absorption, and the paper's own data shows it does not correlate with downstream performance; (4) the exploratory methods (UAD, DFDA) are underpowered proof-of-concepts with limited generalization evidence; (5) the dead feature ratio (89-99%) indicates severe training problems that may invalidate all trained-SAE results. The paper's strongest aspect is its honest negative result reporting, but the framing as a 'reframing' contribution may be overstated.

findings.description: E1 architecture comparison is massively confounded: JumpReLU is pretrained on Gemma data with d_SAE=24,576 while TopK is trained from scratch on OpenWebText with d_SAE=16,384. The 4x collision rate difference (15.4% vs 3.8%) cannot be attributed to architecture alone---it confounds architecture, training data, dictionary size, and training procedure. The paper acknowledges this in Section 4.2 ('this confound prevents definitive comparison') but still presents the 4x difference as the key finding in the Abstract, Introduction, and Conclusion without adequate caveats.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) retrain JumpReLU from scratch on OpenWebText with matched hyperparameters, or (b) demote the architecture comparison to an exploratory observation and reframe the paper around the sparsity sweep (E2/E3) which at least controls for architecture. The Abstract should not lead with the 4x difference.

findings.description: Dead feature ratio of 89-99% across all trained SAEs indicates catastrophic training failure. With 96-99% dead features, the SAE is effectively operating with only 160-640 active features out of 16,384. This is not a 'training problem that may confound results'---it likely invalidates the entire trained-SAE branch of experiments. A 99.4% dead feature ratio at k=10 means the SAE has ~100 active features, making collision rate measurements on 26 first-letter concepts statistically fragile.
findings.suggestion: Investigate why dead feature ratios are so high. Possible causes: insufficient training tokens (1M may be too few), learning rate too high, or SAELens training configuration issues. Re-train with longer training (10M+ tokens), lower learning rate, and monitor dead feature ratio during training. If dead features cannot be reduced below 50%, the trained-SAE experiments should be excluded from the paper.

findings.description: H2-H4 are all falsified (near-zero correlations, p > 0.86), yet the paper frames this as a 'reframing' contribution ('collision rate is a poor proxy') rather than acknowledging that the experiments may simply be underpowered or the wrong measurement. With n=5-6 data points, the study has ~20% power to detect medium effects. The null results are consistent with both (a) collision rate truly not mattering, and (b) the study being too small to detect an effect. The paper leans toward interpretation (a) without adequately considering (b).
findings.suggestion: Add a Bayesian analysis or equivalence testing to distinguish 'no effect' from 'insufficient power.' Report the minimum detectable effect size given the sample size. Consider bootstrapping confidence intervals for the correlation coefficients. Be more explicit that the null results are inconclusive, not conclusive evidence that collision rate doesn't matter.

findings.description: The collision rate metric itself is problematic. It measures whether multiple concepts activate the same feature, but this is not the same as Chanin et al.'s absorption (parent suppressing child under co-occurrence). The paper concedes this ('collision rate is a simplified proxy') but still builds the entire CAAB benchmark around it. A feature shared by 5 letters (feature 18486: c, i, o, p, u) may simply be a polysemantic feature, not an absorbed one. Without parent-child hierarchy testing, calling this 'absorption' is misleading.
findings.suggestion: Rename CAAB to 'Cross-Architecture Collision Benchmark' to be honest about the metric. Implement true Chanin et al. absorption detection for at least the first-letter hierarchies (vowel -> {a,e,i,o,u}) to validate that collision correlates with true absorption. Report the correlation between collision rate and true absorption rate.

findings.description: E2 (causal impact) uses only 5 k-values as the independent variable, but these 5 points are not independent---they come from the same training run with different k. The 'causal' claim is weak because k directly controls both sparsity and reconstruction quality, creating a structural confound. The paper notes that reconstruction MSE 'strongly predicts accuracy' (rho_S = -1.0), which is expected since k directly determines both MSE and sparsity. This is not a causal design; it's a parameter sweep.
findings.suggestion: Reframe E2 as a 'sparsity sweep' rather than a 'causal impact' experiment. Remove claims of 'causal assessment' from contributions. Alternatively, fix reconstruction quality across k values by training to a target MSE rather than fixed k.

findings.description: The exploratory methods (UAD and DFDA) are severely underpowered. UAD is validated on only 25 true collisions from first-letter features---a tiny, homogeneous test set. DFDA is evaluated on only 4 pairs, all sharing the same feature (18486), with one pair showing degradation (-21.4%). The 11.1% 'improvement' is on per-pair residual MSE at 10^-6 scale, not overall reconstruction MSE. The paper correctly notes these are 'proof-of-concept' but still lists them as two of four contributions.
findings.suggestion: Demote UAD and DFDA from 'contributions' to 'exploratory directions' or 'future work.' The current framing overstates their maturity. At minimum, validate UAD on a more diverse set of collisions (not just first letters) and test DFDA on more than one shared feature.

findings.description: Single-seed experiments (seed=42) with no replication. All correlation p-values hover around 0.87, which is suspiciously uniform and suggests the results are driven by a single outlier or random noise pattern. With n=5-6 and one seed, the results may not replicate even on the same setup with a different seed. The pilot summary notes 30.8% collision rate for TopK, but the full experiment shows 3.8%---a 8x difference that is never explained.
findings.suggestion: Run at least 3 seeds for the key experiments (E1-E3) and report standard errors. Explain the 8x discrepancy between pilot (30.8%) and full (3.8%) TopK collision rates---this is a red flag for measurement instability.

findings.description: The core novelty claim---that collision rate differs by architecture and may be a poor proxy---is weakened by the confounded comparison. If the 4x difference is driven by training data/width rather than architecture, the 'cross-architecture benchmark' contribution evaporates. The paper's actual novel contribution may be limited to: (a) showing that collision rate on first-letter features does not correlate with probing accuracy on the same features (a narrow negative result), and (b) two underpowered proof-of-concept methods. This may not meet the bar for a top-tier ML conference.
findings.suggestion: Consider reframing the paper as a 'negative result' or 'methodological caution' paper rather than a benchmark contribution. The honest reporting of null results is the paper's strongest asset. Alternatively, fix the confounds and collect stronger evidence.

findings.description: The specificity scores in f1_caab_results.json are numerically unstable. For JumpReLU, mean specificity is 6.56e8, driven by division-by-near-zero (letters with zero 'other_activation' produce specificity values like 1.14e9). This metric is not meaningful and should not be reported in Table 1.
findings.suggestion: Remove specificity scores from Table 1 or replace with a bounded metric (e.g., clipped at 100). The current values are numerical artifacts, not interpretable statistics.

findings.description: The Abstract claims '11.1% MSE improvement' for DFDA but the paper text correctly says '11.1% per-pair residual MSE improvement.' The Abstract omits the critical 'per-pair residual' qualifier, inflating the claim. Similarly, the outline's Abstract says '11.1% MSE improvement' without the qualifier.
findings.suggestion: Fix the Abstract to match the paper text: '11.1% per-pair residual MSE improvement.'

findings.description: Section 3.6 claims 'approximately 20% power to detect a medium effect size (r = 0.5).' This is a post-hoc power analysis, which is methodologically questionable---power should be computed before the experiment. Reporting post-hoc power can be misleading because it is mathematically related to the p-value (low p implies high post-hoc power, and vice versa).
findings.suggestion: Replace post-hoc power with confidence intervals for the correlation coefficients. Report the minimum effect size detectable with 80% power given n=5-6, to contextualize the null results.

findings.description: The paper mentions Gemma-2-2B as the primary model in the outline and methodology, but all experiments use GPT-2 Small. The reason ('GemmaScope experiments were blocked by API issues') is buried in Section 3.3. This is a significant deviation from the planned methodology that should be more prominent.
findings.suggestion: Move the Gemma-2-2B fallback explanation to the Introduction or a dedicated 'Experimental Constraints' paragraph. Make it clear upfront that all results are on GPT-2 Small.

findings.description: The concept set is limited to 26 first-letter features. This is a very narrow semantic domain---letters are syntactic, not semantic, concepts. The generalization to 'downstream interpretability tasks' is tenuous when the only task is first-letter classification. The paper acknowledges this as a limitation but still frames the results broadly.
findings.suggestion: Add at least one additional concept domain (e.g., numbers or colors) to validate that findings generalize beyond first letters. Or explicitly scope the claims to 'first-letter feature detection' rather than 'downstream interpretability tasks.'

findings.description: The E3 sparsity sweep shows a non-monotonic relationship (k=50 has 0% collision, k=100 has 15.4%), but with n=5 and large random variation, this pattern may be noise. The paper interprets it as 'non-linear' without considering that k=50's 0% could be a sampling fluke. The pilot showed 30.8% collision at k=25, which is inconsistent with the full experiment's 11.5% at k=25.
findings.suggestion: Run multiple seeds for the sparsity sweep and report error bars. The non-monotonic pattern may disappear with replication.

findings.description: The Conclusion says 'Collision rate is a poor proxy for absorption harm' but the paper never actually measures 'absorption harm'---it measures sparse probing accuracy on first-letter features, which is a very specific task. The leap from 'no correlation with first-letter probing' to 'collision rate is a poor proxy for absorption harm' is an overgeneralization.
findings.suggestion: Soften the claim: 'Collision rate does not correlate with first-letter sparse probing accuracy on GPT-2 Small, suggesting it may not be a reliable proxy for this specific downstream task.'

metric_gaming_risks: Collision rate is not true absorption---the paper measures feature sharing, not parent-child suppression. Calling it 'absorption' inflates the apparent relevance.
DFDA's 11.1% improvement is on per-pair residual MSE at 10^-6 scale, not overall reconstruction MSE. The absolute improvement is negligible (order 10^-7) but framed as '11.1% improvement.'
UAD's 100% recall is achieved by flagging all same-cluster pairs---with 54.3% precision, nearly half are false positives. Perfect recall is trivially achievable with a permissive threshold.
The 4x architecture difference is the paper's headline result, but it confounds at least 3 variables (architecture, training data, dictionary size).
Dead feature ratios of 89-99% mean the SAEs are not functioning as intended, yet results are presented as meaningful.
novelty_risks: The cross-architecture comparison is confounded, undermining the core CAAB contribution.
The 'collision rate is a poor proxy' finding is based on underpowered experiments (n=5-6) and may not replicate.
UAD and DFDA are proof-of-concepts with minimal validation; similar approaches may exist in the literature (co-occurrence clustering for feature analysis is not new).
Chanin et al. (2024) already documented absorption; this paper's contribution is measuring a proxy metric (collision) across architectures, which is incremental if the comparison is confounded.
The negative result (H2 falsified) is valuable but may not be sufficient as a standalone contribution for a top-tier venue.
reproducibility_gaps: Single seed (42) with no replication; results may not generalize to other seeds.
Pilot and full experiment show 8x discrepancy in TopK collision rate (30.8% vs 3.8%) with no explanation.
Pretrained JumpReLU from GemmaScope may not be reproducibly loadable (API issues noted).
Dead feature ratios of 89-99% suggest training configuration is unstable; reproducing the exact dead feature ratio may be difficult.
No code repository URL provided ('[anonymous repository]' placeholder in Conclusion).
UAD validation depends on supervised labels from first-letter features, which are domain-specific and may not generalize.
